sessions.title: Paper 2 - Preparation Session 1
sessions.subtitle: Build a real, working AI in your browser with Google Teachable Machine - no coding needed.
sessions.intro: In this session you will build a complete, working AI system from start to finish: a 'Recycling Sorter' that looks through a camera and decides whether it is seeing Paper, Plastic, or Metal. You will do every stage of a real Paper 2 project - define the problem, collect and clean data, train a model, test it, improve it, and save your evidence. Follow every step exactly. Each step has a picture of the screen you should be looking at.

overview.label: Tool you will use
overview.value: Google Teachable Machine - a free website that trains AI models in your browser. No programming, no installing anything.

overview.label: What you will build
overview.value: A 3-class image classifier (Paper / Plastic / Metal) - the classic safe project that still shows the full AI pipeline.

overview.label: Time needed
overview.value: About 2-3 hours. You can pause after any step and come back later.

overview.label: What it covers
overview.value: All of Paper 2 Component A (building the AI system) and it produces the screenshots you need for Component B (the report).

materials: A laptop or desktop with the Google Chrome browser and a working internet connection.
A webcam - the one built into most laptops is fine. (A phone camera also works if you upload photos instead.)

Objects to photograph: a few real items for each group - some paper/card, some plastic items (bottle, wrapper), and some metal items (can, spoon). 3-5 real objects per group is enough to take ~40 photos of each.
A free Google account, so you can save your project to Google Drive and not lose your work.
An empty folder on your computer named 'Paper2 Evidence' to keep every screenshot you take.

steps.title: Open Teachable Machine and start an Image Project
steps.detail: This is the website where you will build the AI. You do not need to log in or download anything to start.
substeps: Open Google Chrome and go to the address: teachablemachine.withgoogle.com
Click the big 'Get Started' button.
A box appears asking what kind of project you want. Click 'Image Project'.
A second box asks for the type of image model. Click 'Standard image model'.
steps.tip: If you get a pop-up asking to use your camera, click 'Allow'. Without this the webcam will not work later.
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#eef2ff'/><rect x='40' y='30' width='720' height='380' rx='14' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#c7d2fe' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='40' y='30' width='720' height='46' rx='14' fill='#e0e7ff'/><circle cx='68' cy='53' r='6' fill='#f87171'/><circle cx='90' cy='53' r='6' fill='#fbbf24'/><circle cx='112' cy='53' r='6' fill='#34d399'/><rect x='140' y='40' width='560' height='26' rx='13' fill='#ffffff'/><text x='158' y='58' font-size='14' fill='#64748b'>teachablemachine.withgoogle.com</text><text x='400' y='118' font-size='22' fill='#1e293b' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>New Project</text><rect x='120' y='150' width='170' height='175' rx='14' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='3'/><text x='205' y='235' font-size='44' text-anchor='middle'>📷</text><text x='205' y='290' font-size='16' fill='#3730a3' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Image Project</text><rect x='315' y='150' width='170' height='175' rx='14' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='235' font-size='44' text-anchor='middle'>🎤</text><text x='400' y='290' font-size='16' fill='#94a3b8' text-anchor='middle'>Audio Project</text><rect x='510' y='150' width='170' height='175' rx='14' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='595' y='235' font-size='44' text-anchor='middle'>🏃</text><text x='595' y='290' font-size='16' fill='#94a3b8' text-anchor='middle'>Pose Project</text><circle cx='120' cy='150' r='16' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='120' y='156' font-size='15' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>1</text><text x='205' y='360' font-size='14' fill='#ef4444' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Click this, then 'Standard image model'</text></svg>

steps.title: Get to know the 3 areas of the screen
steps.detail: Before you touch anything, understand the layout. The screen is split into three columns, and you always work from left to right.
substeps: On the LEFT are your 'Classes' - the groups the AI learns to tell apart. This is where you add your example photos.
In the MIDDLE is the 'Training' box with the big 'Train Model' button. This is where the AI actually learns.
On the RIGHT is the 'Preview' box. This is where you test the finished AI live with your camera.
steps.tip: Think of it as a factory line: put examples in on the left, train in the middle, test the result on the right.

fill='#ef4444' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>PREVIEW (testing)</text></svg>

steps.title: Name your three classes
steps.detail: The AI needs to know which groups it is learning. Your problem is: 'sort recycling into Paper, Plastic, or Metal', so you need three classes with those names.
substeps: The page starts with two classes called 'Class 1' and 'Class 2'. Click on the name 'Class 1' (or the small pencil icon) and type: Paper
Click on 'Class 2' and rename it to: Plastic
Click the '+ Add a class' button at the bottom to create a third class, and name it: Metal
Double-check you now have exactly three classes: Paper, Plastic, Metal.
steps.tip: Clear class names are worth marks - the examiner sees you defined the problem properly (Component A: 'clear problem definition').
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='250' y='40' width='300' height='72' rx='12' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='274' y='82' font-size='18' fill='#1e293b' font-weight='bold'>Paper</text><text x='372' y='82' font-size='16' fill='#94a3b8'>✎</text><rect x='250' y='124' width='300' height='72' rx='12' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='274' y='166' font-size='18' fill='#1e293b' font-weight='bold'>Plastic</text><text x='390' y='166' font-size='16' fill='#94a3b8'>✎</text><rect x='250' y='208' width='300' height='72' rx='12' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='3'/><text x='274' y='250' font-size='18' fill='#1e293b' font-weight='bold'>Metal</text><text x='378' y='250' font-size='16' fill='#94a3b8'>✎</text><rect x='250' y='300' width='300' height='54' rx='12' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#c7d2fe' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><text x='400' y='333' font-size='16' fill='#4f46e5' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>+  Add a class</text><circle cx='250' cy='208' r='16' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='250' y='214' font-size='15' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>3</text><text x='585' y='250' font-size='13' fill='#ef4444' font-weight='bold'>new 3rd class</text><path d='M575 240 L560 240' stroke='#ef4444' stroke-width='2'/><text x='585' y='330' font-size='13' fill='#ef4444' font-weight='bold'>click to add</text></svg>

steps.title: Collect example photos for each class
steps.detail: Now you teach the AI by giving it lots of examples. You do this one class at a time. The webcam is the fastest way.
substeps: Click inside the 'Paper' class, then click the 'Webcam' button. Your camera turns on.
Hold a piece of paper in front of the camera. Press and HOLD the 'Hold to Record' button - it keeps taking photos while you hold it.
While holding, slowly move and rotate the paper so you capture different angles. Aim for about 40-50 photos.
Do the exact same for 'Plastic' (hold plastic items) and 'Metal' (hold metal items).
No webcam? Click 'Upload' instead and add photos from your computer or Google Drive.
steps.tip: Change the angle, distance and background between shots. A model trained on only one pose only recognises that one pose.
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='60' y='40' width='680' height='360' rx='14' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><text x='84' y='78' font-size='18' fill='#1e293b' font-weight='bold'>Paper</text><rect x='84' y='96' width='120' height='34' fill='#334155' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>48 Image Samples</text><g fill='#93c5fd'><rect x='370' y='166' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='432' y='166' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='494' y='166' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='556' y='166' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='618' y='166' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='370' y='228' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='432' y='228' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='494' y='228' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='556' y='228' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='618' y='228' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='370' y='290' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='432' y='290' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/><rect x='494' y='290' width='52' height='52' rx='6'/></g><circle cx='209' cy='352' r='16' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='209' y='358' font-size='15' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>4</text><text x='530' y='330' font-size='13' fill='#ef4444' font-weight='bold'>photos collect here as you hold</text></svg>

steps.title: Clean your data so the classes are fair
steps.detail: The quality of your photos decides how good your AI is. This step is worth 10 marks on its own (Component A: 'data preparation'), so do not rush it.
substeps: Check the number of samples on each class. They should be roughly EQUAL (for example ~45 each). If one class has far fewer, add more photos to it.
Look through the photos. Hover over any that are blurry, too dark, or where the object is out of frame, and click the delete (X) to remove them.
Make sure each class has variety: different backgrounds, lighting and angles - not 45 identical shots.
Write one sentence in your notes explaining WHY balanced, clean data matters (the examiner rewards this).
steps.tip: Unbalanced data makes the AI biased toward the class with the most photos - exactly the kind of point that also earns marks in your report's ethics section.
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#f8fafc'/><g><rect x='40' y='60' width='210' rx='13' fill='#dcfce7'/><text x='201' y='92' font-size='13' fill='#166534' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>45</text></g><g><rect x='295' y='60' width='210' height='320' rx='12' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='315' y='92' font-size='16' fill='#1e293b' font-weight='bold'>Plastic</text><rect x='425' y='74' width='62' height='26' rx='13' fill='#dcfce7'/><text x='456' y='92' font-size='13' fill='#166534' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>47</text></g><g><rect x='550' y='60' width='210' height='320' rx='12' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='570' y='92' font-size='16' fill='#1e293b' font-weight='bold'>Metal</text><rect x='680' y='74' width='62' height='26' rx='13' fill='#dcfce7'/><text x='711' y='92' font-size='13' fill='#166534' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>46</text></g><g fill='#93c5fd'><rect x='60' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='108' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='156' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='60' y='168' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/></g><rect x='108' y='168' width='40' height='40' rx='5' fill='#94a3b8'/><line x1='112' y1='172' x2='144' y2='204' stroke='#ffffff' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='144' y1='172' x2='112' y2='204' stroke='#ffffff' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='150' cy='168' r='13' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='150' y='173' font-size='13' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>X</text><text x='128' y='250' font-size='12' fill='#ef4444' font-weight='bold'>delete blurry ones</text><g fill='#93c5fd'><rect x='315' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='363' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='411' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='570' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='618' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/><rect x='666' y='120' width='40' height='40' rx='5'/></g><text x='400' y='410' font-size='14' fill='#ef4444' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Keep the counts balanced and remove poor photos</text></svg>

steps.title: Train the model
steps.detail: This is the moment the AI actually learns from your photos. It only takes a minute or two.
substeps: Click the big 'Train Model' button in the middle Training box.
A progress bar appears. IMPORTANT: do not switch to another browser tab or close the window while it trains - that stops the training.
Wait until it says the model is trained. You do not need to change the 'Advanced' settings (epochs, batch size) - the defaults are fine for now.
Take a screenshot of the finished training screen for your report.
steps.tip: If you ever add or delete photos later, you must click 'Train Model' again for the changes to take effect.
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='250' y='120' width='300' height='200' rx='16' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='162' font-size='18' fill='#1e293b' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Training</text><rect x='285' y='185' width='230' height='46' rx='12' fill='#4f46e5'/><text x='400' y='214' font-size='16' fill='#ffffff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Train Model</text><rect x='285' y='250' width='230' height='16' rx='8' fill='#e2e8f0'/><rect x='285' y='250' width='150' height='16' rx='8' fill='#4f46e5'/><text x='400' y='294' font-size='13' fill='#64748b' text-anchor='middle'>Training... 65%</text><rect x='230' y='340' width='340' height='44' rx='10' fill='#fef2f2' stroke='#fecaca' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='367' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>⚠ Do NOT switch tabs while training</text><circle cx='285' cy='185' r='16' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='285' y='191' font-size='15' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>6</text></svg>

steps.title: Test your AI with items it has never seen
steps.detail: Now find out if it actually works. The right-hand Preview box runs your live AI.
substeps: Look at the 'Preview' box on the right. Make sure 'Input' is set to 'Webcam' and switched ON.
Hold up an object - ideally a NEW one you did not use in training (this proves the AI really learned, not memorised).
Watch the coloured bars. The AI shows how confident it is for each class - the longest bar is its answer.
Try all three types. Take screenshots showing correct predictions for your report.
steps.tip: Testing on unseen items is the whole point of 'generalisation'. If it only works on your training objects, it has memorised, not learned.
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='250' y='40' width='300' height='360' rx='16' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='74' font-size='16' fill='#1e293b' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Preview</text><rect x='280' y='90' fill='#0f172a'/><rect x='345' y='170' width='110' height='75' rx='6' fill='#e2e8f0'/><text x='400' y='212' font-size='12' fill='#64748b' text-anchor='middle'>a NEW plastic bottle</text><text x='285' y='312' font-size='13' fill='#334155' font-weight='bold'>Output</text><text x='285' y='334' font-size='12' fill='#475569'>Paper</text><rect x='340' y='324' width='150' height='12' rx='6' fill='#e2e8f0'/><rect x='340' y='324' width='16' height='12' rx='6' fill='#93c5fd'/><text x='498' y='334' font-size='11' fill='#64748b'>7%</text><text x='285' y='356' font-size='12' fill='#475569'>Plastic</text><rect x='340' y='346' width='150' height='12' rx='6' fill='#e2e8f0'/><rect x='340' y='346' width='132' height='12' rx='6' fill='#4f46e5'/><text x='498' y='356' font-size='11' fill='#4f46e5' font-weight='bold'>88%</text><text x='285' y='378' font-size='12' fill='#475569'>Metal</text><rect x='340' y='368' width='150' height='12' rx='6' fill='#e2e8f0'/><rect font-size='14' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>7</text><text x='620' y='210' font-size='13' fill='#ef4444' font-weight='bold'>longest bar =</text><text x='620' y='230' font-size='13' fill='#ef4444' font-weight='bold'>the AI answer</text></svg>

steps.title: Improve the model and record before-and-after
steps.detail: No first model is perfect. Finding a weakness and fixing it is worth 10 marks (Component A: 'evaluation and improvements').
substeps: Note down where it gets confused - for example, shiny plastic mistaken for metal.
Write down the accuracy you see BEFORE improving (roughly how often it is right out of 10 tries).
Fix the weak class: add 10-15 more varied photos to it (different lighting/angles), then click 'Train Model' again.
Test again and write down the accuracy AFTER. Keep both numbers - a before/after comparison is exactly what the examiner wants.
steps.tip: One clear, measured improvement beats many vague changes. 'I added 15 photos of shiny plastic and accuracy rose from 7/10 to 9/10' is a top-mark sentence.
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='120' y='60' width='560' height='320' rx='16' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='100' font-size='18' fill='#1e293b' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>My accuracy</text><line x1='180' y1='340' x2='620' y2='340' stroke='#cbd5e1' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='240' y='230' width='90' height='110' rx='6' fill='#94a3b8'/><text x='285' y='215' font-size='18' fill='#334155' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>70%</text><text x='285' y='365' font-size='14' fill='#64748b' text-anchor='middle'>BEFORE</text><rect x='470' y='140' width='90' height='200' rx='6' fill='#4f46e5'/><text x='515' y='125' font-size='18' fill='#4f46e5' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>92%</text><text x='515' y='365' font-size='14' fill='#64748b' text-anchor='middle'>AFTER</text><path d='M340 250 Q400 170 460 190' stroke='#22c55e' stroke-width='3' fill='none' stroke-dasharray='6 5'/><text x='400' y='160' font-size='13' fill='#16a34a' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>+15 photos, retrained</text></svg>

steps.title: Save and export your model
steps.detail: Keep your work safe and get a shareable version of your AI for the presentation.
substeps: First, save the whole project: click the menu (top-left) and choose 'Save project to Drive'. This lets you reopen it later.
To share the working AI, click the 'Export Model' button (top-right of the Preview box).
In the box that opens, keep the 'Tensorflow.js' tab selected and click 'Upload my model'.
It gives you a shareable link. Copy it - anyone can open that link and use your AI in their browser. Save the link for your presentation.
steps.tip: The shareable link is perfect evidence for Component C (presentation) - you can demo the live AI from any computer without rebuilding it.
steps.illustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 800 440' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='Arial, sans-serif'><rect width='800' height='440' fill='#f8fafc'/><rect x='170' y='40' width='460' height='360' rx='16' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='78' font-size='18' fill='#1e293b' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Export your model</text><rect x='200' y='100' width='130' height='34' rx='8' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#6366f1' stroke-width='2'/><text x='265' y='122' font-size='12' fill='#4f46e5' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Tensorflow.js</text><rect x='336' y='100' width='120' height='34' rx='8' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='396' y='122' font-size='12' fill='#94a3b8' text-anchor='middle'>Tensorflow</text><rect x='462' y='100' width='140' height='34' rx='8' fill='#ffffff' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='532' y='122' font-size='12' fill='#94a3b8' text-anchor='middle'>Tensorflow Lite</text><rect x='200' y='158' width='250' height='40' rx='10' fill='#4f46e5'/><text x='325' y='183' font-size='14' fill='#ffffff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>Upload my model</text><text x='200' y='240' font-size='13' fill='#334155' font-weight='bold'>Your shareable link:</text><rect x='200' y='254' width='400' height='38' rx='8' fill='#f1f5f9' stroke='#cbd5e1' stroke-width='2'/><text x='214' y='278' font-size='12' fill='#4f46e5'>teachablemachine.withgoogle.com/models/AbCd12/</text><rect x='200' y='320' width='400' height='50' rx='10' fill='#ecfdf5' stroke='#a7f3d0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='350' font-size='13' fill='#047857' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>💾 Also: menu → 'Save project to Drive'</text><circle cx='200' cy='158' r='16' fill='#ef4444'/><text x='200' y='164' font-size='14' fill='#fff' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>9</text></svg>

steps.title: Turn it into your Paper 2 evidence
steps.detail: You have now built a working AI. This final step converts what you did into the marks for the report (Component B) and presentation (Component C).
substeps: Collect the screenshots you took: your labelled classes, the sample photos, the training screen, the correct predictions, the before/after accuracy, and the export link.

In your report, write: (1) the objective, (2) the step-by-step process, (3) your results, (4) an ETHICS section - e.g. the model may be biased if it only saw clean items in good light, and using a camera raises privacy questions.
Prepare a short slide deck or screen recording that demonstrates the live AI using your shareable link.
You are done with Project 1! You have completed a full AI pipeline end-to-end.
steps.tip: Do the ethics section properly - bias, privacy and who is affected. It is easy marks that weak projects skip.

Objective</text><rect x='268' y='114' width='264' height='10' rx='5' fill='#e2e8f0'/><rect x='268' y='130' width='210' height='10' rx='5' fill='#e2e8f0'/><text x='268' y='168' font-size='13' fill='#4f46e5' font-weight='bold'>2. Process (screenshots)</text><rect x='268' y='180' width='80' height='56' rx='6' fill='#c7d2fe'/><rect x='356' y='180' width='80' height='56' rx='6' fill='#c7d2fe'/><rect x='444' y='180' width='80' height='56' rx='6' fill='#c7d2fe'/><text x='268' y='272' font-size='13' fill='#4f46e5' font-weight='bold'>3. Results (before / after)</text><rect x='268' y='282' width='264' height='10' rx='5' fill='#e2e8f0'/><text x='268' y='320' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='bold'>4. Ethics: bias & privacy</text><rect x='268' y='330' width='264' height='10' rx='5' fill='#fee2e2'/><rect x='268' y='346' width='230' height='10' rx='5' fill='#fee2e2'/><rect x='268' y='378' width='264' height='30' rx='8' fill='#ecfdf5' stroke='#a7f3d0' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='398' font-size='12' fill='#047857' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='bold'>+ slides / recording for the demo</text></svg>

evidence: Screenshot: your three named classes (Paper / Plastic / Metal) with their sample photos.
Screenshot: the training screen after 'Train Model' finished.
Screenshot: at least three correct live predictions in the Preview box.
Your before-and-after accuracy numbers, with a note on what you changed.
The shareable export link to your working model.
A short written report including an ethics section, plus slides or a screen recording for the demo.
sessions.ethics_note: For the report's ethics section, think about: bias (if you only photographed clean items in bright light, the AI may fail on dirty or dark real recycling); privacy (a camera could capture people, not just objects); and who is affected if the sorter gets it wrong. Naming these earns real marks and shows mature understanding.
sessions.next_up: Next session will be a brand-new end-to-end project that teaches you more - for example a sound or text classifier, or your first Python model in Google Colab. Each project builds on the skills from this one.
